retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Action Potential -- Related Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Conduction velocity tests, specifically within the peripheral nerves, can determine if there are deficits in the transmission of action potentials. However, further testing is required to identify the specific mechanism(s) of conduction block or decreases in conduction velocity. [11] For example, a decrease in conduction velocity may be due to injured axons followed by remyelination with short internode length, nerve constriction as observed in carpal tunnel syndrome, or axonal tapering in the distal limbs. [5] [11] Additionally, injury to the nerve, diabetic neuropathy, or demyelination caused by autoimmune disorders such as multiple sclerosis or Guillain–Barré syndrome could decrease the speed or even block conduction of electrical signals within the nerves. [14] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Unmyelinated Nerve Fibers -- Structure and Function -- Conduction Properties of C-fibers
retrieved_docs.content: Unmyelinated C tactile fibers are slowly conducting. Their optimal response is through slow stroking stimulation with a velocity of 1 to 10cm/s (approximately 1 m/s). This signal produces the less well-localized, burning "second" pain after the Ad-fiber-related (approximately 15 m/s) pricking "first" pain. [6] In unmyelinated fibers, conduction velocity is proportional to the square root of axonal diameter, which is proportional to the square root of channel density. This square root of channel density approximates the longitudinal number of sodium ion channels across the axon. On the other hand, conduction velocity approximates axonal diameter (not square root) in myelinated fiber sheathes. However, myelin in very thin fibers does not increase the conduction velocity. [7] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Electrodiagnostic Evaluation of Ulnar Neuropathy -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Physiology of Nerve Conduction
retrieved_docs.content: Unmyelinated fibers conduct in the range of 1 to 5 m/sec. On the other hand, myelinated motor and sensory nerve axons have conduction velocities up to 150 m/sec. This process is called saltatory conduction. Myelin is produced by Schwann cells, which are concentrically wrapped around axons. These myelin sheaths have gaps called nodes of Ranvier, where action potentials occur and propagate quickly until the next node is met. Therefore, current flows passively and jumps from node to node.

retrieved_docs.title: Nerve Conduction Studies and Electromyography  -- Technique or Treatment -- Motor Nerve Conduction Studies
retrieved_docs.content: Prolonged latency suggests a demyelinating injury. The CMAP amplitude is typically measured from the baseline to the negative peak and is expressed in millivolts. CMAP serves as a semiquantitative measure of the number of conducting axons between the electrodes, with reduced amplitude indicating axonal injury. CMAP duration refers to the duration of the evoked potential and is measured in milliseconds, reflecting the conduction rate of the various axons between the measuring electrodes. Conduction velocity measures the speed of conduction and is expressed in meters per second. A reduced conduction velocity suggests demyelinating injury. [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Electrodiagnostic Evaluation of Acute Inflammatory Demyelinating Polyneuropathy -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Physiology of Nerve Conduction
retrieved_docs.content: Unmyelinated fibers conduct in the range of 1 to 5 meters per second. On the other hand, myelinated motor and sensory nerve axons have conduction velocities up to 150 meters per second. This process is called saltatory conduction. Myelin production occurs via Schwann cells, which concentrically wrap around axons. These myelin sheaths have gaps called nodes of Ranvier, where action potentials occur and propagate quickly until encountering the next node. Therefore, current flows passively and jumps from node to node.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Nerve -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Nerve impulses are dependent on the ability of the resulting action potential to propagate down the length of the axon without losing amplitude. This process differs in myelinated and unmyelinated axons. In unmyelinated axons, the original action potential results in the inflow of sodium ions. These sodium ions will be repelled from each other and attracted to the nearby more negative section of the axon, which causes depolarization there as well, thus opening of adjacent voltage-gated ion channels, allowing more sodium ions to enter, further depolarizing along the length of the axon. The speed of propagation is dependent on the rate of depolarization of the segment of axon in front of the action potential. This speed gets influenced by the concentration of the sodium channels and the diameter of the axon. The larger the axon, the less internal resistance there is to ion flow, thus there is greater conduction and faster nerve impulses. The presence of myelin greatly increases conduction velocity. Myelin decreases the capacitance, which is the ability to store charge, of the section of the axon it covers. If the section of the axon cannot store charge, more charge is spread along the rest of the axon, increasing the rate of depolarization and speed of propagation. Myelin is wrapped so tightly around the axon that ion flow across the axon membrane cannot occur, otherwise known as increased transmembrane resistance, which is why myelinated axons only develop action potentials at small gaps in myelin called nodes of Ranvier. Once an action potential develops at a node, the current travel quickly along the myelinated section to the next node, where another action potential gets generated. These jumps in action potentials in myelinated axons are termed saltatory conduction. Myelinated axons have developed specific characteristics to maximize effectiveness. Voltage-gated sodium channels are much denser at nodes of Ranvier and sparse to absent under the myelin sheath. This allows the neuron to focus its energy on opening and synthesizing channels in the nodes rather than wasting resources in the myelinated section where ions need to propagate along the axon. [10] [11] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Sensory System -- Cellular Level -- Nerve Fiber Classification
retrieved_docs.content: The nerve fiber types along which action potentials propagate are classified according to diameter and conduction velocity, with an Erlanger Gasser classification (A-C) as well as a numerical classification (I-IV). From A (largest, myelinated, fastest) to C (smallest, unmyelinated, slowest), nerve fiber diameter decreases, as does relative conduction velocity.

retrieved_docs.title: Electrodiagnostic Evaluation of Acute Inflammatory Demyelinating Polyneuropathy -- Indications -- Nerve conduction studies
retrieved_docs.content: Conduction velocity: The speed of the fastest conducting motor axon, which tends to be prolonged in demyelinating disorders. Amplitude: Voltage difference from baseline to maximal negative peak with depolarization, a reflection of intact, non-diseased muscle fibers that can depolarize Latency: Reflects the speed of neurotransmission and is defined as the time from stimulus to initial CMAP deflection from baseline. Duration: Reflects the synchronous transmission of action potentials; it can give a global evaluation of the motor fiber conduction, with many fibers slowing conduction, affecting the duration of action potentials (measured by the time from an initial deflection from baseline to first crossing) [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Foot Drop -- Evaluation -- Nerve Conduction Studies
retrieved_docs.content: Nerve conduction studies assess the viability of myelin. Nerve compression is indicated by a delay in conduction latency. Findings such as delayed latency, slow velocity, and conduction block at the affected segment suggest nerve compression.

retrieved_docs.title: Electrodiagnostic Evaluation of Ulnar Neuropathy -- Indications -- Nerve Conduction Studies
retrieved_docs.content: Conduction velocity: The speed of the fastest conducting motor axon; tends to be prolonged in demyelinating disorders Amplitude: Voltage difference from baseline to maximal negative peak with depolarization, a reflection of intact, nondiseased muscle fibers that can depolarize Latency: Reflects the speed of neurotransmission; defined as the time from stimulus to initial CMAP deflection from baseline Duration: Reflects the synchronous transmission of action potentials; can give a global evaluation of the motor fiber conduction, with many fibers slowing conduction, affecting the duration of action potentials; measured as the time from an initial deflection from baseline to the first crossing.

retrieved_docs.title: Electrodiagnostic Evaluation of Acute Inflammatory Demyelinating Polyneuropathy -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Physiology of Nerve Conduction
retrieved_docs.content: During AIDP, the autoimmune target can predict the electrodiagnostic findings, prognosis, and clinical presentation. The most common target is the myelinated sheaths across the axon and within Schwann cells, leading to decreased conduction velocity. [4] Although less common, nerve axon fibers can also be targets. This activity leads to axonal injuries that can decrease action potential amplitudes and lead to abnormal nerve-muscle connections.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Nerve -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Neurophysiologically, with nerve conduction studies, the axonal neuropathy is characterized by loss of nerve action potential amplitude, whereas demyelinating neuropathy is characterized by an early decrease in nerve conduction velocities.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Unmyelinated Nerve Fibers -- Structure and Function -- Conduction Properties of C-fibers
retrieved_docs.content: This conduction velocity equation is based on the new conductive model discussed in the study by Akaishi and colleagues (2017). [5] The differences in intracellular ionic concentration gradients around several ion (voltage-gated sodium, NaV) channels cause membrane charges across an axon. By increasing the Coulomb force between electrolytes from the inflowing sodium ions through voltage-gated sodium channels, this ionic migration and conduction propagation generates the action potential at each channel. This increasing Coulomb force is inversely proportional to the sodium channel density on the axon membrane. Continuous transmission of this action potential thus causes nerve conduction in unmyelinated axons.

retrieved_docs.title: Electrodiagnostic Evaluation of Peroneal Neuropathy -- Clinical Significance -- Motor Nerve Conduction Studies
retrieved_docs.content: A reduction in fibular CMAP amplitudes (ankle, below the lateral popliteal fossa or fibular head) indicates an axonal loss. This is because the pickup is measured at a distal muscle (EDB muscle) whose nerve has experienced Wallerian degeneration. The motor nerve's conduction velocity and the distal latency can either be normal or slightly slowed, depending on whether there is a loss of the fastest conducting axons. The patient usually experiences a combination of axonal loss and demyelination in the injured area.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Withdrawal Response -- Related Testing
retrieved_docs.content: The withdrawal reflex can be tested using an electromyogram (EMG). This device measures the electrical activity of peripheral nerves and striated skeletal muscles and includes a needle EMG and nerve conduction studies. The EMG was first described in 1943 by Weddell et al, who pioneered its use in examining muscles. [10] Nerve conduction studies provide an efficient method of quantifying the conduction velocity (CV) of nerves and the amplitude of sensory nerve action potentials (SNAPs) and compound motor action potentials. The nerve CV reflects the speed of propagation of action potentials by saltatory conduction along myelinated axons in a peripheral nerve. SNAP amplitude is determined by the number of axons in a sensory nerve, while the amplitude of compound muscle action potential reflects an integrated function of the motor axons, neuromuscular junction, and striated muscle. Needle EMGs are utilized to detect myopathic changes in muscle in response to denervation. Very fine needles are inserted into several muscles, each with a microscopic electrode that can pick up muscles' normal and abnormal electrical signals.

retrieved_docs.title: Electrodiagnostic Evaluation of Peripheral Neuropathy -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: EDX can be performed to understand the etiology, severity, prognosis, and possible treatment options for peripheral neuropathy. It can identify the primary characteristics of the neuropathy as axonal, demyelinating, sensory, motor, multifocal, or diffuse; however, there is often overlap. [20] Classic NCS findings for demyelinating injury include prolonged distal latency, slowed conduction velocity, and a prolonged F-wave latency. Demyelination generally occurs at many sites along a nerve, causing variation in nerve action potential propagation, such as a slowed conduction velocity or a conduction block. Slowed conduction velocity in a non-uniform pattern causes abnormal dispersion of arrival times of nerve action potentials at the recording site, referred to as abnormal temporal dispersion. Conduction block refers to a blockage of many axons over a short nerve segment. It is most frequently encountered at sites of entrapment in mononeuropathies, such as in carpal tunnel syndrome or peroneal neuropathy at the knee. In peripheral neuropathies, focal conduction block can be evident in a site unaffected by entrapment and may represent focal demyelination. It is also important to note that slowed conduction may occur from reversible metabolic causes with no myelin damage. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2239
retrieved_docs.content: Hemorrhage into nerve trunk, inflammatory and toxic neuropathy, and hereditary sensory neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب الحسي الوراثي) are all conditions in which the nerve serves as the primary site of pathological findings. This highlights that the nerve is a common locus for diverse neuropathic processes, ranging from acute hemorrhagic injury to inflammatory, toxic, and genetic sensory neuropathies. Recognizing the nerve as the affected structure helps clinicians focus diagnostic evaluation and tailor management strategies for patients presenting with these nerve‑related disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2193
retrieved_docs.content: Other disorders of nervous system constitute a broad diagnostic category that encompasses a variety of distinct neuropathic and neuromuscular conditions. Within this category, اعتلال الأعصاب السكري refers to diabetic neuropathy, a common complication of chronic hyperglycemia that can cause sensory loss, pain, and autonomic dysfunction. اعتلال الأعصاب المترقي مجهول السبب designates an idiopathic progressive neuropathy of unknown cause, characterized by gradual worsening of peripheral nerve function. اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الوعائي الكولاجيني describes multiple neuropathies that occur secondary to collagen vascular diseases, linking systemic autoimmune inflammation with peripheral nerve damage. اعتلال الأعصاب بالغ الخطورة denotes a severe form of neuropathy in which extensive nerve injury leads to profound motor and sensory deficits. اعتلال العصب والجذور المتعدد الالتهابي مزيل للميالين identifies a demyelinating inflammatory polyneuropathy, in which immune‑mediated attack on myelin sheaths produces widespread weakness and paresthesia. الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي refers to reflex sympathetic dystrophy of the lower limb (complex regional pain syndrome type I), a painful autonomic disorder that follows trauma or surgery. Finally, وهن عضلي وبيل captures a muscular weakness/atrophy syndrome, reflecting primary muscle involvement that may coexist with or mimic peripheral nerve disease. Recognizing that all of these entities are classified under Other disorders of nervous system aids clinicians in organizing differential diagnoses, selecting appropriate investigations, and tailoring multidisciplinary management strategies for affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 181
retrieved_docs.content: diseases, genetic neuropathies, and distinct syndromes. This unified structure supports coding, diagnosis, and patient‑centered management of a broad array of neurological conditions, emphasizing the central role of peripheral nerves, pain, paralysis, and targeted surgical decompression in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2703
retrieved_docs.content: Peripheral nerve decompression is directly related to متلازمة النفق الرسغي (carpal tunnel syndrome), as the surgical procedure aims to relieve pressure on the median nerve within the carpal tunnel, thereby alleviating the pain, numbness, and functional impairment characteristic of متلازمة النفق الرسغي. This relationship highlights the role of decompression techniques in the effective management of this common entrapment neuropathy and underscores their importance for improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 2664
retrieved_docs.content: Elektrostimulační terapie, a therapeutic modality that applies electrical currents to stimulate nerves, muscles, or other tissues, is broadly related to إرحال أيوني, reflecting its influence on ionic transport processes within cells and across membranes. This relationship highlights how electrostimulation can modulate ion channel activity, alter membrane potentials, and affect physiological functions such as muscle contraction, nerve conduction, and tissue repair. Recognizing the connection between elektrostimulační terapie and إرحال أيوني is essential for optimizing treatment protocols, anticipating therapeutic outcomes, and integrating electrostimulation strategies into comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 184
retrieved_docs.content: peripheral‑nerve disease. Collectively, these relationships depict “Other disorders of nervous system” as an integrative framework that connects diverse neuropathic, vascular, inflammatory, toxic, traumatic, hereditary, and musculoskeletal entities to their anatomical sites, diagnostic findings (including abnormal brain evoked‑potential maps), and therapeutic interventions, thereby guiding clinicians in the comprehensive recognition and management of nervous‑system disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2411
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Other disorders of nervous system” serves as an umbrella classification for a diverse group of neuropathic conditions. It includes polyneuropathy that results from medication use, multiple‑nerve‑bundle neuropathies that occur in malignant diseases, and similar multifocal neuropathies associated with collagen‑vascular disorders. Severe or high‑risk neuropathies are also placed within this group, as are chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathies that involve both nerves and roots and lead to demyelination. Mononeuritis, a single‑nerve inflammation, and lower‑limb reflex sympathetic dystrophy (a painful, reflex‑driven condition of the extremities) are likewise classified under “Other disorders of nervous system.” Additionally, the term “تكزز” is listed as a subclass within this broader category, reflecting the inclusion of various language‑specific descriptors for these neurological disorders. Together, these classifications highlight the wide spectrum of peripheral and central nervous system pathologies that are grouped under “Other disorders of nervous system,” aiding clinicians in diagnosis, coding, and management of these heterogeneous conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 264
retrieved_docs.content: arteriopathy, autosomal dominant, with subcortical infarcts and leukoencephalopathy type 1, which manifests as gait disorder (اضطراب المِشْيَة) and migraine. Familial hemiplegic migraine (شقيقة فالجية عائلية) represents a hereditary form, and personal‑history documentation of migraine (H/O: migraine) is mapped to the patient’s neurological and sensory disease history. The peripheral nerve is described as a central anatomic site for neuropathic, inflammatory, metabolic and neuromuscular disorders. Surgical incision of a nerve is a subprocedure of peripheral nerve decompression, routinely employed for entrapment syndromes such as carpal‑tunnel syndrome and tarsal‑tunnel syndrome and refined to carpal‑tunnel release. Inflammatory and toxic neuropathies, hereditary sensory neuropathy, chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy, diabetic neuropathy, idiopathic progressive neuropathy, severe neuropathy, drug‑induced polyneuropathy, malignant‑ or collagen‑vascular‑associated

This framework supports precise anatomical localization, differential diagnosis, and therapeutic planning, especially the central role of nerve decompression procedures across multiple anatomical sites.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 128

This interconnected framework supports comprehensive documentation, precise diagnosis, and interdisciplinary communication for patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 1239
retrieved_docs.content: Hemorrhage into nerve trunk, inflammatory and toxic neuropathy, and the hereditary sensory neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب الحسي الوراثي) are all clinical entities that have the nerve as their finding site, indicating that pathological changes in these conditions are localized to nerve tissue. Likewise, an incision of nerve is a direct procedural intervention performed on the nerve, underscoring the nerve as a common target for both disease processes and surgical treatment. Recognizing that the nerve serves as the anatomical locus for these diverse findings and procedures helps clinicians accurately diagnose neuropathic conditions, plan appropriate interventions, and coordinate patient care.
